Node.js Basics, Part 9: Creating a Firebase Project
Since we'll probably be building a few things with Firebase coming up, we need to get the basics down first.

It's one of Google's products — an app platform that supports Android, iOS, and web development all at once. Pretty powerful stuff. What we'll mainly use here is its database, Firebase, which is a cloud-based NoSQL database.
So what's NoSQL? The concept varies a bit depending on who's defining the rules, so I'll just borrow a quick summary from Wikipedia. In short, NoSQL means
you don't use SQL commands as the query language
— which is why you'll sometimes see people refer to NoSQL as "Not Only SQL."
NoSQL also scales really well. When your data fills up, you just add more machines to scale out. With SQL it's more complicated — you'd need to do partitioning and other adjustments.
Mine looks a little different since I've already created Firebase projects before
When you first land, it'll probably ask you to create a project — just fill in the fields as prompted.
The creation process takes about one to two minutes.
Once you're in, you'll see this screen ↓
Seeing this screen means you've created it successfully

Since we'll be building a few things with Firebase coming up, we need to understand the basics first. This post covers what Firebase is, a quick word on NoSQL, and how to create your first Firebase project.
